title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
3.2 Measures of Centre



Measure
Formula




Mean
\(\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i\)


Median
Middle value (ordered)


Mode
Most frequent value

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Measures of Spread



Measure
Formula




Variance
\(s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2\)


Std Dev
\(s = \sqrt{s^2}\)


Range
\(x_{\max} - x_{\min}\)


IQR
\(Q_3 - Q_1\)

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Percentiles & Quartiles

\(Q_1\) — 25th percentile
\(Q_2\) — 50th percentile (median)
\(Q_3\) — 75th percentile
\(\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1\) (middle 50% of data)

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
3.3 Histograms

Divides range into equal-width bins
Height = frequency or density
Shows shape: symmetric, skewed, bimodal

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Boxplots
Displays the five-number summary:
\[\min,\ Q_1,\ \text{median},\ Q_3,\ \max\]
Points beyond \(1.5 \times \text{IQR}\) are plotted as outliers.

title: Chapter 9: Simple Linear Regression
text:
9.1 The Linear Model
\[Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\]

\(\beta_0\) — intercept
\(\beta_1\) — slope
\(\varepsilon_i\) — random error

title: Chapter 9: Simple Linear Regression
text:
Least Squares Estimates
\[\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}\]
\[\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1\bar{x}\]

title: Chapter 9: Simple Linear Regression
text:
Partitioning Variability
\[SS_T = SS_R + SS_E\]
\[R^2 = \frac{SS_R}{SS_T} \in [0,1]\]
\(R^2\) = proportion of variability in \(Y\) explained by \(x\).

title: Chapter 9: Simple Linear Regression
text:
9.2 Inference
\[T = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1 - \beta_{1,0}}{SE(\hat{\beta}_1)} \sim t_{n-2}\]
\[\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{SS_E}{n-2}\]

title: Chapter 9: Simple Linear Regression
text:
ANOVA Table for SLR



Source
df
MS
F




Regression
1
\(MS_R\)
\(MS_R/\hat{\sigma}^2\)


Error
\(n-2\)
\(\hat{\sigma}^2\)



Total
\(n-1\)

title: Chapter 9: Simple Linear Regression
text:
Residual Plots
Plot \(\hat{\varepsilon}_i\) vs \(\hat{y}_i\).

Random scatter → assumptions satisfied
Fan shape → non-constant variance
Curve → non-linear relationship

title: Chapter 10: Analysis of Categorical Data
text:
10.1 Multinomial Model
Outcomes fall into \(k\) categories:
\[p_1 + p_2 + \cdots + p_k = 1\]
Observed counts \(n_1, \ldots, n_k\) with \(\sum n_i = n\).

title: Chapter 10: Analysis of Categorical Data
text:
10.2 Goodness of Fit Test
\[H_0: p_i = p_{i,0} \text{ for all } i\]
Test statistic:
\[\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^k \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}\]
where \(E_i = n \cdot p_{i,0}\).

title: Chapter 10: Analysis of Categorical Data
text:
Decision Rule
\[\chi^2 \sim \chi^2_{k-1} \text{ under } H_0\]
Reject \(H_0\) if \(\chi^2 > \chi^2_{\alpha,\, k-1}\).

title: Chapter 10: Analysis of Categorical Data
text:
10.3 Sample Size Assumption






Warning


All expected counts must satisfy \(E_i \geq 5\).



If violated: - Combine small categories - Use Fisher’s exact test

title: Chapter 10: Analysis of Categorical Data
text:
Summary

Categorical data → count-based methods
Goodness of fit: does data match a given distribution?
Chi-square test is approximate — check \(E_i \geq 5\)

title: Chapter 2: Introduction to R
text:
Why R?

Free and open-source
Designed for statistics
Huge package ecosystem (CRAN)
Industry and academia standard

title: Chapter 2: Introduction to R
text:
2.2 Setup

Install R → cran.r-project.org
Install RStudio → posit.co

title: Chapter 2: Introduction to R
text:
2.3 R as a Calculator
2 + 3       # 5
10 / 4      # 2.5
sqrt(16)    # 4
log(100, base = 10)  # 2

title: Chapter 2: Introduction to R
text:
Data Structures
x  <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)              # vector
m  <- matrix(1:9, nrow = 3)         # matrix
df <- data.frame(x = 1:3,
                 y = c("a","b","c")) # data frame

title: Chapter 2: Introduction to R
text:
Basic Functions
mean(x)
sd(x)
summary(x)
length(x)

title: Chapter 2: Introduction to R
text:
Histogram
hist(x,
     main = "Histogram",
     xlab = "Values",
     col  = "steelblue")

title: Chapter 2: Introduction to R
text:
Packages
install.packages("ggplot2")
library(ggplot2)






Tip


Only run install.packages() once. Always run library() at the top of each script.

title: Chapter 5: Confidence Intervals
text:
What is a Confidence Interval?
A range of plausible values for an unknown parameter.
\[\text{estimate} \pm \text{margin of error}\]

title: Chapter 5: Confidence Intervals
text:
5.2 Interpretation
A 95% CI means: across many samples, 95% of intervals constructed this way will contain the true parameter.






Warning


The parameter is fixed. The interval is random — not the other way around.

title: Chapter 5: Confidence Intervals
text:
5.3 One Sample CIs







Parameter
Formula




Mean (\(\sigma\) unknown)
\(\bar{x} \pm t_{\alpha/2,n-1} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\)


Proportion
\(\hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}\)


Variance
\(\left(\frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2}},\ \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2}}\right)\)

title: Chapter 5: Confidence Intervals
text:
5.4 Two Sample CIs







Parameter
Formula




\(\mu_1 - \mu_2\)
\((\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm t_{\alpha/2} \cdot \text{SE}\)


\(p_1 - p_2\)
\((\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2) \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \text{SE}\)


\(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2\)
\(\left(\frac{s_1^2}{s_2^2 F_{\alpha/2}},\ \frac{s_1^2 F_{\alpha/2}}{s_2^2}\right)\)

title: Chapter 5: Confidence Intervals
text:
5.5 Paired Data
\[\bar{d} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot \frac{s_d}{\sqrt{n}}, \quad d_i = x_{1i} - x_{2i}\]

title: Chapter 5: Confidence Intervals
text:
5.6 Sample Size
For a mean: \(\quad n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma}{E}\right)^2\)
For a proportion: \(\quad n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}}{E}\right)^2 \hat{p}(1-\hat{p})\)

title: Chapter 8: Analysis of Variance
text:
When to Use ANOVA
Comparing means across 3 or more groups.
\[H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \cdots = \mu_k\] \[H_1: \text{at least one mean differs}\]

title: Chapter 8: Analysis of Variance
text:
The ANOVA Table



Source
SS
df
MS
F




Treatment
SST
\(k-1\)
MST
\(\frac{\text{MST}}{\text{MSE}}\)


Error
SSE
\(N-k\)
MSE



Total
SSTot
\(N-1\)

title: Chapter 8: Analysis of Variance
text:
Assumptions

Independence — observations are independent
Normality — each group is approximately normal
Equal variances — \(\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = \cdots = \sigma_k^2\)

title: Chapter 8: Analysis of Variance
text:
Decision Rule
Reject \(H_0\) if:
\[F > F_{\alpha,\, k-1,\, N-k}\]






Note


Rejecting \(H_0\) tells you some means differ — not which ones. Use post-hoc tests (e.g. Tukey) to identify which.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text: Slides PDF

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
Summary

Statistics = collecting, organizing, and interpreting data under uncertainty
Categorical data: labels (e.g. colour, yes/no)
Discrete data: countable numbers (e.g. number of students)
Continuous data: measured values (e.g. height, temperature)
A sample is used to make conclusions about a population
Always comes with uncertainty — that’s the point of inference

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Power
text:
7.2 Error Types




\(H_0\) True
\(H_0\) False




Reject \(H_0\)
Type I (\(\alpha\))
Correct ✓


Fail to Reject
Correct ✓
Type II (\(\beta\))

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Power
text:
Power
\[\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ false})\]

Higher power → better chance of detecting a real effect
Typical target: Power \(\geq 0.80\)

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Power
text:
What Increases Power?

Larger sample size \(n\)
Larger effect size \(\delta\)
Larger \(\alpha\) (less strict)
Smaller \(\sigma\) (less variability)

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Power
text:
7.3 Sample Size for Power
\[n = \left(\frac{(z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)\,\sigma}{\delta}\right)^2\]
\(\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_0\) is the minimum effect size worth detecting.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
What is Statistics?
The science of collecting, organizing, analyzing, and interpreting data to make decisions under uncertainty.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
1.1 Foundations

Population — the full set of individuals of interest
Sample — a subset of the population we observe
Parameter — a numerical summary of the population
Statistic — a numerical summary of the sample

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
1.2 Types of Data

Categorical — qualitative labels (e.g. gender, colour)
Discrete — countable values (e.g. number of defects)
Continuous — measured on a scale (e.g. height, temperature)

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
1.3 Inferential Statistics
We use a sample to make conclusions about a population.
\[
\text{Sample} \xrightarrow{\text{inference}} \text{Population}
\]
Key tools: estimation, hypothesis testing, confidence intervals.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
Key Takeaways

Statistics bridges data and decisions
Know whether your data is categorical or numerical
Inference always involves uncertainty — quantify it

title: Chapter 4: Foundations of Inference
text:
4.1 Key Distributions



Distribution
Notation
Use




Normal
\(N(\mu, \sigma^2)\)
General


Chi-Square
\(\chi^2(k)\)
Variance tests


t
\(t(k)\)
Mean, unknown \(\sigma\)


F
\(F(d_1, d_2)\)
Variance ratios

title: Chapter 4: Foundations of Inference
text:
Arithmetic of Random Variables
If \(X \perp Y\) and both normal:
\[X + Y \sim N(\mu_X + \mu_Y,\ \sigma_X^2 + \sigma_Y^2)\]

title: Chapter 4: Foundations of Inference
text:
4.2 Sampling Distributions
The distribution of a statistic across repeated samples.
\[\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)\]
Standard error: \(\text{SE}(\bar{X}) = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\)

title: Chapter 4: Foundations of Inference
text:
4.3 Central Limit Theorem
For large \(n\), regardless of population shape:
\[\bar{X} \xrightarrow{d} N\!\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)\]






Tip


Rule of thumb: \(n \geq 30\) is usually sufficient.

title: Chapter 4: Foundations of Inference
text:
4.4 Law of Large Numbers
\[\bar{X}_n \xrightarrow{p} \mu \quad \text{as } n \to \infty\]
More data → sample mean gets closer to the true mean.

title: Chapter 6: Hypothesis Tests
text:
The Testing Framework

State \(H_0\) and \(H_1\)
Choose significance level \(\alpha\)
Compute the test statistic
Find the p-value
Reject \(H_0\) if p-value \(< \alpha\)

title: Chapter 6: Hypothesis Tests
text:
6.2 One Sample Tests








Parameter
Test Statistic
Distribution




Mean
\(T = \frac{\bar{X}-\mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}}\)
\(t_{n-1}\)


Proportion
\(Z = \frac{\hat{p}-p_0}{\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}}\)
\(N(0,1)\)


Variance
\(\chi^2 = \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma_0^2}\)
\(\chi^2_{n-1}\)

title: Chapter 6: Hypothesis Tests
text:
6.3 Two Sample Tests







Parameter
Test Statistic




\(\mu_1 - \mu_2\)
\(T = \frac{(\bar{X}_1-\bar{X}_2)-\delta_0}{\text{SE}}\)


\(p_1 - p_2\)
\(Z = \frac{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}{\text{SE}_{\text{pool}}}\)


\(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2\)
\(F = s_1^2/s_2^2\)

title: Chapter 6: Hypothesis Tests
text:
6.4 Paired Data
\[T = \frac{\bar{d} - \delta_0}{s_d/\sqrt{n}} \sim t_{n-1}\]
Use when observations are naturally matched (e.g. before/after).

title: Chapter 6: Hypothesis Tests
text:
p-value Interpretation






Note


The p-value is the probability of observing results as extreme as ours, assuming \(H_0\) is true. It is not the probability that \(H_0\) is true.
